pokemonChampionsMysteryGiftCodes.title: Pokemon Champions Mystery Gift Codes
pokemonChampionsMysteryGiftCodes.subtitle: Start with the live freebies before spending VP. The launch pool includes one redeemable code and two no-code bonuses.
pokemonChampionsMysteryGiftCodes.intro: Mystery Gifts are the fastest early boost in Pokemon Champions because they can add real roster value without any VP cost. Finish the tutorial and set up your first team, then open Submenu > Mystery Gifts and claim redeemed rewards from the Mailbox.

items.howToGet: Redeem with Code/Password in the Mystery Gifts menu, then claim it from the Mailbox.
items.whyItMatters: This is the main launch code and the only public redeemable code confirmed right now.

items.howToGet: Play Pokemon Champions during the early-download period.
items.whyItMatters: Dragonite is a premium early roster piece, and the Quick Coupons help cycle recruitment faster.

items.howToGet: Play 3 battles in the Warm-Up Challenge event.
items.whyItMatters: This is the best short-term free pickup because it adds both a usable early meta Pokemon and recruitment acceleration.

pokemonChampionsBeginnerGuide.title: Pokemon Champions Beginner Guide
pokemonChampionsBeginnerGuide.subtitle: Pokemon Champions is a battle-first game, so your first wins come from understanding roster building, VP flow, and format basics. Use this as the shortest path from tutorial to a functional ranked team.
pokemonChampionsBeginnerGuide.intro: Unlike the mainline games, Pokemon Champions throws you straight into competitive structure. The early game is about picking a workable lineup, earning VP, recruiting smartly, and learning how Singles and Doubles battles actually play out.

steps.title: Pick the lineup, not just the lead
steps.description: Your first starter choice comes with five fixed teammates, so the full package matters more than the face Pokemon.
details: Starter lineups are fixed, not random.
Dragonite can be added immediately to every starter lineup.
A strong support cast can matter more than the lead mon itself.

steps.title: Learn the battle structure first
steps.description: The game has Ranked Battles, Casual Battles, and Private Battles, and each can be played in Single or Double Battle format.
details: Ranked Battles are the main progression lane.
Ranked results affect your rank, season rewards, and long-term ladder growth.
Pokemon Champions is the official VGC software for Worlds 2026.

steps.title: Treat VP as your main progression currency
steps.description: Victory Points are the backbone of the game because they power both recruitment and training.
details: VP comes from Ranked Battles and other in-game sources.
Spend early VP on lasting roster value before luxury upgrades.

steps.title: Use Roster Ranch every day
steps.description: Recruitment is how you build your roster if you are not importing from Pokemon HOME.
details: You recruit from a random roster using VP.
A fresh lineup appears without VP every 22 hours.
Trial Recruitment can be used once a day without VP.

steps.title: Build around roles, not just favorites
steps.description: A stable team needs physical pressure, special pressure, and support utility instead of six independent attackers.
details: Training lets you adjust stats, Abilities, and moves.
Physical attackers, special attackers, and support mons each fill essential roles.

steps.title: Use Mega Evolution and HOME as your major power spikes
steps.description: The first Ranked ruleset allows Mega Evolution, and Pokemon HOME can instantly widen your roster if you already own eligible mons.
details: Mega Evolution is enabled under the first Ranked rules.
The Omni Ring is the battle device tied to Mega Evolution.
Only Pokemon that appear in Pokemon Champions can visit from Pokemon HOME.

pokemonChampionsStarterTeams.title: Pokemon Champions Starter Teams
pokemonChampionsStarterTeams.subtitle: Every starter unlocks a full fixed lineup, so your first choice shapes your early ladder experience more than it would in a traditional Pokemon game.
pokemonChampionsStarterTeams.intro: Starter teams are one of the biggest early-game decisions because they determine which six-mon shell you begin with. If you want the safest general pick, Gardevoir is the standout; if you want raw team strength for Singles, Pikachu is the most explosive package; if you want Doubles control, Tyranitar is the cleanest opener.

items.whyPick: This is the most consistent starter for most players because the lead is strong immediately and the supporting cast stays useful in early competitive play.
Pikachu itself is not the star; the reason to pick it is the loaded support lineup, which is one of the strongest ready-made starter squads in the game.
Sand Stream, Intimidate support, Whimsicott utility, and Sylveon spread pressure give this lineup the cleanest early Doubles game plan.
Palafin rewards players who know how to trigger Hero Form efficiently, and the supporting cast adds immediate offensive pressure.
Snorlax is the forgiving option for players who want a bulkier lead and a lower-pressure way to learn the game.
A comfortable starter for players who want straightforward pressure, strong speed, and familiar offensive pieces.
This lineup leans harder into offensive pressure and gives you a more explosive opening shell than the safer utility starts.
Lucario's package covers a lot of ground and stays playable, but it is not as immediately dominant as the top three starter choices.
This lineup is attractive because it bundles several high-value support and pressure pieces into one early pick.

items.bestFor: Players who want a broad utility spread
items.whyPick: Altaria's team gives you multiple physical threats and a usable toolset across different matchups, even if it is not the strongest meta opener.

pokemonChampionsTierList.title: Pokemon Champions Tier List
pokemonChampionsTierList.subtitle: The launch meta is still settling, but a handful of Pokemon already stand out as the most reliable pieces for ladder play. These are the strongest early anchors to recruit, train, or build around first.
pokemonChampionsTierList.intro: Early Pokemon Champions team building is less about finding one perfect six and more about locking in a few proven cores. Garchomp and Gardevoir define a lot of early matchups, Dragonite stays premium in both normal and Mega play, and bulky pieces like Hippowdon and Incineroar shape the slower side of the meta.

sTier.whyItWins: Garchomp brings Earthquake pressure, broad Ground/Rock/Dragon coverage, and Rough Skin chip, making it one of the central early-meta threats.
sTier.howToGet: Available immediately through the Pikachu starter lineup.

sTier.whyItWins: Gardevoir surged early because it checks common Dragon pressure, fits fast offensive teams, and can run Telepathy in Doubles to pair safely with ally spread moves.
sTier.howToGet: Available through the Gardevoir starter lineup and the live Warm-Up Challenge reward.

sTier.whyItWins: Dragonite is both a strong base mon and a premium Mega option, with Extreme Speed utility and natural synergy beside Earthquake users.
sTier.howToGet: Every starter path can add Dragonite immediately, and it is also part of the early-download bonus.

sTier.whyItWins: Hippowdon's Ground typing, Sand Stream, Yawn, and Slack Off make it one of the cleanest early defensive anchors for slower teams.
sTier.howToGet: Recruit from Roster Ranch or bring it in from HOME if eligible.

sTier.whyItWins: Incineroar keeps its classic VGC value thanks to Intimidate, Fake Out, and Parting Shot, making it one of the easiest support slots to justify.
sTier.howToGet: Recruit or transfer it, then train it into your preferred support spread.

aTier.whyItWins: Mega Charizard Y and Drought-driven Fire pressure keep Charizard near the front of the early offensive meta.
aTier.howToGet: Available from the Charizard starter lineup.

aTier.whyItWins: Archaludon is one of the best defensive picks at launch, with huge staying power, many resistances, and enough move flexibility to anchor slower teams.
aTier.howToGet: Rare through recruitment unless you already own one from Scarlet and Violet.

aTier.whyItWins: Meowscarada stands out for elite speed, flexible builds, and the ability to threaten a wide range of targets before they move.
aTier.howToGet: Recruit or bring it in from HOME if you already have one.

aTier.whyItWins: Hydreigon's rare Dark/Dragon typing, high Special Attack, and U-turn utility make it one of the better offensive recruits to build around early.
aTier.howToGet: Available through Palafin and Armarouge starter lineups, or via recruitment.

aTier.whyItWins: Greninja keeps value through elite speed, broad move access, and Protean-driven flexibility, even if it stays fragile.
aTier.howToGet: Recruit it, or move it in through HOME if you already own one.

pokemonChampionsBestTeams.title: Pokemon Champions Best Replica Teams to Copy
pokemonChampionsBestTeams.subtitle: These are the most practical early teams to build around if you want ready-made lineups instead of starting from scratch. Each card shows the full six-Pokemon shell, the replica code, and the playstyle it supports.
pokemonChampionsBestTeams.intro: Replica Teams are the fastest way to jump into serious battles with a proven lineup. The strongest early teams already center on recurring staples like Charizard, Incineroar, Garchomp, Gengar, Palafin, and Archaludon, with both weather offense and control styles represented.

items.best_for: A flexible first replica team with immediate Mega pressure, speed control, and solid defensive pivots.
Players who want a fast weather team with immediate pressure and strong utility support.
A quick ladder team that leans on strong early pressure and fast revenge options.
A rounded weather shell for players who want rain support without giving up utility and speed options.
A slower control lineup built around setting Trick Room and letting heavy hitters take over.

pokemonChampionsRankedBattlesGuide.title: Pokemon Champions Ranked Battles Guide
pokemonChampionsRankedBattlesGuide.subtitle: Ranked Battles are the main long-term progression loop for competitive players. This table covers formats, VP gains, season structure, and the current launch-season setup.
pokemonChampionsRankedBattlesGuide.intro: Pokemon Champions is built around competitive battling, and Ranked Battles sit at the center of that loop. You climb by playing against trainers of similar skill, earn VP after matches, and push through rotating seasonal regulations that reshape the legal roster over time.

items.details: Ranked Battles, Casual Battles, and Private Battles all support both Single Battle and Double Battle formats.
Your rank changes with your results, and Ranked Battles are designed to match you against trainers of similar skill.
Victory Points are used to recruit and train Pokemon, and VP cannot be purchased directly.
Players begin at Poke Ball Tier, Rank 4, then climb through sub-ranks and higher tiers as wins stack up.
Mega Evolution is enabled in Ranked Battles at launch through the Omni Ring.
Final placement, final rank, and season rewards are all determined from your season results.
Eligible Pokemon and other battle parameters change when a new regulation set begins.
The current launch season runs from April 8, 2026 at 02:00 UTC to June 17, 2026 at 01:59 UTC under Regulation Set M-A.
Season Points are earned through Ranked Battles and other play, and both SP and the Battle Pass reset each season.
Starting in 2026, Pokemon Champions is the VGC software for the Pokemon World Championships and qualifying Championship Series play.

pokemonChampionsPokemonHomeGuide.title: Pokemon Champions Pokemon HOME Guide
pokemonChampionsPokemonHomeGuide.subtitle: Pokemon HOME is one of the defining features of Champions because it lets you bring in eligible partners from other games. The key is knowing the exact transfer path and the rules that apply once a Pokemon becomes a visitor.
pokemonChampionsPokemonHomeGuide.intro: Pokemon Champions uses Pokemon HOME as a bridge between your older collections and the new battle-focused roster. The workflow is straightforward once linked, but visitors follow game-specific rules for move legality, storage, and return transfers.

steps.title: Link the same Nintendo Account
steps.description: Use the same Nintendo Account for the Nintendo Switch version and mobile device version of Pokemon HOME so both versions can access the same stored Pokemon.

steps.title: Open the Champions connection in HOME
steps.description: On Nintendo Switch, select the Pokemon Champions icon from the HOME screen. On mobile, go to Options and use Send for a Visit in the Link with Pokemon Champions section.

steps.title: Choose only eligible visitors
steps.description: Only Pokemon that appear in Pokemon Champions can visit. If a species is not supported by the current Champions roster, it cannot be sent in.

steps.title: Send and collect in Champions
steps.description: After sending a visitor from HOME, go into Pokemon Champions, open Recruit, and use Collect from Pokemon HOME to bring that Pokemon into your Champions box.

steps.title: Fix moves through training
steps.description: If a visiting Pokemon knows moves that cannot be used in Pokemon Champions, retrain that moveset inside Champions before using it in battle.

steps.title: Understand what can return
steps.description: Visiting Pokemon can be sent back to Pokemon HOME, but Pokemon obtained inside Pokemon Champions cannot be deposited into Pokemon HOME.

steps.title: Keep the training rules in mind
steps.description: Training done in Pokemon Champions does not carry over to Pokemon HOME. If the same visitor comes back to Champions later, its Champions training is preserved.

steps.title: Use HOME to manage limited storage
steps.description: Champions starts with a compact box setup, so HOME is the easiest way to rotate older favorites in and out while keeping your battle-ready roster focused.

pokemonChampionsAllPokemonList.title: Pokemon Champions All Pokemon List
pokemonChampionsAllPokemonList.subtitle: Roster intent is strong because every transfer, team plan, and upgrade decision depends on what is actually usable. This overview gives the launch-size snapshot, notable forms, and the biggest availability rules players need to know first.
pokemonChampionsAllPokemonList.intro: Pokemon Champions launched with a sizable but curated roster built for competitive play rather than full National Dex coverage. Mega Evolutions are already a major part of the roster, several regional and special forms are supported, and a few important transfer restrictions shape what players can actually bring in.

items.notes: The game currently lists 187 Pokemon species in its available roster.
Mega forms are tracked separately from the base species list.
Pokemon outside the current roster need an event or transfer route to be obtained.
It is legal under the current regulation but must be transferred from Pokemon Legends: Z-A through HOME.

items.value: Alolan Raichu, Alolan Ninetales, Hisuian Arcanine, Hisuian Decidueye
items.notes: Permanent forms are listed alongside their base species in the roster.

items.value: Incineroar, Meowscarada, Palafin, Farigiraf, Kingambit, Archaludon
items.notes: Many of the most searched battle-ready names are already present in the launch-era pool.

items.value: Mega Charizard X, Mega Charizard Y, Mega Blastoise, Mega Dragonite, Mega Feraligatr
items.notes: The launch Mega pool includes both classic Mega staples and newly supported evolutions.
The launch roster contains no Legendary or Mythical Pokemon.

items.value: Battle Bond Greninja cannot be sent
items.notes: Some special variants remain excluded even when the base species family is supported.
Pawmot appears in the game data but cannot currently be transferred in.

pokemonChampionsTrainingGuide.title: Pokemon Champions Training Guide
pokemonChampionsTrainingGuide.subtitle: Train Your Roster Around VP, Roles, and Long-Term Value
pokemonChampionsTrainingGuide.intro: Training in Pokemon Champions is not just a stat screen. VP powers recruitment and customization, and the game also lets you edit moves and Abilities once a Pokemon is permanently on your roster. A good training flow starts with steady VP income, avoids wasting resources on temporary trial recruits, and finishes by aligning stats, nature, and held item choices with the role each Pokemon is meant to play.

steps.title: Build VP income first
steps.description: Ranked Battles award Victory Points after matches, and VP is the core currency used for both recruiting and training Pokemon. VP cannot be purchased directly, so regular battle volume is the foundation of roster growth.

steps.title: Use Roster Ranch efficiently
steps.description: Roster Ranch gives you a free random lineup refresh every 22 hours. You can also use Trial Recruitment once per day without spending VP, which is useful for testing whether a Pokemon fits your team plan before you commit resources.

steps.title: Promote keepers before you invest heavily
steps.description: Trial recruits cannot be trained, so major VP spending should go into Pokemon you plan to keep. Permanent Recruitment uses VP, while Teammate Tickets from in-game missions can permanently recruit a Pokemon without paying VP.

steps.title: Train by battle role, not by habit
steps.description: Each Pokemon gets 66 extra stat points, with a maximum of 32 points in a single stat. Fast sweepers usually prioritize Speed plus one attacking stat, while bulky attackers and tanks lean into HP, Defense, or Special Defense depending on the matchup role they need to cover.

steps.title: Finish the build with nature, moves, ability, and item synergy
steps.description: Pokemon Champions lets you train stats, moves, and Abilities, and Training Tickets can reduce the VP cost of training to zero. After the role is set, complete the build with a nature and held item that fit the plan instead of buying items at random.

pokemonChampionsMovesetsAndBestBuilds.title: Pokemon Champions Movesets and Best Builds
pokemonChampionsMovesetsAndBestBuilds.subtitle: Early Meta Builds to Copy, Compare, and Adapt
pokemonChampionsMovesetsAndBestBuilds.intro: Movesets in Pokemon Champions are not one-size-fits-all. Some Pokemon already have very stable doubles packages, while others split into support, offense, or rain-based variants. This module highlights builds that are easy to understand at a glance, with enough detail for players to decide whether they want a safe ladder setup or a more specialized team slot.

items.format: Doubles core, with a separate singles variant
items.coreBuild: Intimidate | Fake Out / Flare Blitz / Knock Off / U-turn
items.topItems: Assault Vest 35.5%, Safety Goggles 26.0%, Sitrus Berry 24.2%, Rocky Helmet 8.5%
items.commonMoves: Fake Out 99.8%, Flare Blitz 91.0%, Knock Off 84.2%, Parting Shot 62.2%
items.teamNote: Games.gg also highlights a singles build using Impish nature, Leftovers or Sitrus Berry, and Flare Blitz / Parting Shot / Darkest Lariat / Will-O-Wisp.

items.coreBuild: Stamina | Assault Vest | Electro Shot / Flash Cannon / Body Press / Dragon Pulse
items.topItems: Assault Vest 94.1%; Power Herb 4.4% is the main alternative
items.commonMoves: Electro Shot 94.6%, Flash Cannon 80.3%, Body Press 75.4%, Dragon Pulse 55.2%
items.teamNote: Pelipper and Urshifu-Rapid-Strike are the clearest partners, with Amoonguss frequently rounding out the core.

items.coreBuild: Intimidate | Draco Meteor / Protect / Air Slash / Tailwind
items.topItems: Life Orb 28.6%; Choice Scarf, Rocky Helmet, Covert Cloak, Sitrus Berry, and Eject Pack each appear as viable variants
items.commonMoves: Draco Meteor 71.4%, Protect 71.4%, Air Slash 57.1%, Tailwind 42.9%
items.teamNote: A rain-flavored variant also appears with Eject Pack plus Draco Meteor / Hurricane / Hydro Pump / Protect.

items.role: Follow Me support and Beat Up enabler
items.coreBuild: Friend Guard | Follow Me / Protect / Beat Up / Taunt
items.topItems: Wide Lens 23.1%, Rocky Helmet 23.1%, Covert Cloak 15.4%, Focus Sash 15.4%
items.commonMoves: Follow Me 100.0%, Protect 61.5%, Beat Up 46.2%, Taunt 46.2%
items.teamNote: Friend Guard is the main ability at 69.2%, but Technician Population Bomb variants also show up as a more aggressive alternative.

pokemonChampionsMegaEvolutionGuide.title: Pokemon Champions Mega Evolution Guide
pokemonChampionsMegaEvolutionGuide.subtitle: How to Equip Mega Stones and Use Mega Evolution Correctly
pokemonChampionsMegaEvolutionGuide.intro: This guide covers the exact questions most players hit first: where Mega Stones are stored, how to equip them, when the Mega toggle appears in battle, and which stones are already confirmed in the launch shop. Mega Evolution is powerful, but it also costs your held-item slot and can only be activated once per battle, so good execution matters as much as picking the right Mega.

steps.title: Finish the tutorial before trying to use Mega Stones
steps.description: Mega Stones are not usable until the tutorial is complete. Buying one early does not break anything, but the item cannot be equipped until that tutorial gate has been cleared.

steps.title: Open the correct item menu
steps.description: Use either the Box menu and press X for the Held Item screen, or select a Pokemon in your active battle lineup and choose Give an Item to Hold. Mega Stones sit in a separate tab, so they will not show up in the standard inventory view.

steps.title: Match the stone to the Pokemon species
steps.description: Mega Stones are species-specific, such as Gengarite for Gengar. Equipping one also uses the held-item slot, so that Pokemon gives up options like Sitrus Berry, Assault Vest, or Rocky Helmet.

steps.title: Trigger Mega Evolution during the battle
steps.description: When the correct holder is active in battle, choose Fight and press R to toggle Mega Evolution before selecting the move. Only one Pokemon can Mega Evolve per battle, and if that Pokemon switches out later, it stays in Mega form.

steps.title: Prioritize the launch stones that are easy to plan around
steps.description: Confirmed launch-shop examples include Charizardite X, Charizardite Y, Gengarite, Tyranitarite, Scizorite, and Gardevoirite at 2000 VP each. Additional Mega Stones are tied to routes such as the Mega Evolution Tutorial, Battle Pass rewards, Mystery Gifts, and the Frontier Shop.

pokemonChampionsHeldItemsGuide.title: Pokemon Champions Held Items Guide
pokemonChampionsHeldItemsGuide.subtitle: Held Items to Learn First for Faster Team Upgrades
pokemonChampionsHeldItemsGuide.intro: Held items in Pokemon Champions do not all do the same kind of work. Some are cheap matchup tools, some are steady damage boosts, and a few can completely swing a turn when triggered. The fastest way to improve item decisions is to separate baseline shop buys from the utility pieces that should only be purchased for a specific team or matchup.

items.examples: Silk Scarf, Miracle Seed, Charcoal, Mystic Water, Magnet, Dragon Fang, Metal Coat, Fairy Feather
items.whatItDoes: Straightforward offensive shop items listed at 700 VP each.
items.howToUseIt: Best when a Pokemon wins games by repeatedly clicking one main STAB attack type.

items.examples: Cheri, Chesto, Pecha, Rawst, Aspear, Persim
items.whatItDoes: Cheap berry-slot utility items available in the shop.
items.howToUseIt: Useful when one status effect would shut down a key attacker or support slot.

items.examples: Rindo, Occa, Passho, Wacan, Tanga, Coba, Charti, Kebia, Shuca, Yache, Chople, Payapa, Kasib, Haban, Colbur, Babiri, Roseli
items.whatItDoes: Matchup berries listed in the shop for targeted counterplay.
items.howToUseIt: Buy these for specific ladder threats rather than as blind all-purpose items.

items.whatItDoes: Higher-cost niche items already listed in the launch shop.
items.howToUseIt: Worth considering when a specific Pokemon or crit-focused line makes the item slot matter more than raw bulk.

items.whatItDoes: If another Pokemon makes contact with the holder, it takes damage.
items.howToUseIt: Excellent on bulky support Pokemon that expect to absorb physical contact.

items.whatItDoes: Boosts the holder's attack power if it is hit by a move it is weak to.
items.howToUseIt: Best on Pokemon that can survive the trigger turn and immediately convert it into pressure.

items.whatItDoes: Heals the holder each turn if it is a Poison-type, otherwise it takes damage each turn.
items.howToUseIt: Reserve it for Poison-type users only; it is a sustain slot, not a generic recovery item.

items.whatItDoes: Raises the holder's Sp. Atk after it uses a sound-based move.
items.howToUseIt: Strong on special attackers whose move plan naturally includes a sound-based trigger.
A niche utility choice that matters only when you are building for that specific interaction.

pokemonChampionsSeasonsAndRegulations.title: Pokemon Champions Seasons and Regulations
pokemonChampionsSeasonsAndRegulations.subtitle: Pokemon Champions separates short ranked seasons from longer regulation windows. Your season determines rank results and season rewards, while regulations determine the usable roster and battle parameters for multiple seasons.
pokemonChampionsSeasonsAndRegulations.intro: This is the quick reference block for players who want to know what is live right now and what changes next. In the current launch period, Regulation M-A is the active ruleset, Season M-1 is the first ranked season, and Play! Pokemon competition support is moving onto Pokemon Champions during late May and beyond.

items.label: Launch Day
items.title: Season M-1 and the first Battle Pass begin
items.details: Ranked Battle Season M-1 began on April 8, 2026 at 02:00 UTC. The first seasonal Battle Pass also opened on the same day, tying the opening reward track to the first ranked season.

items.label: Current Regulation
items.title: Regulation M-A is the active launch ruleset
items.details: Regulation M-A is the first regulation group in Pokemon Champions and runs from April 8 to June 17, 2026. Regulations control the usable Pokemon pool and other battle parameters across multiple seasons.

items.label: Season Change
items.title: Season M-1 ends before the regulation window does
items.details: Season M-1 and its Battle Pass run until May 13, 2026 at 01:59 UTC. This means the first season ends while Regulation M-A is still active, showing how seasons rotate faster than regulations.

items.label: Premier Event Transition
items.title: Pokemon Champions becomes the Play! Pokemon event platform
items.details: Pokemon Champions makes its Play! Pokemon Premier Event debut at the Indianapolis Regional Championships on May 29-31, 2026. It remains the platform for later Premier Events including the Turin Special Event on June 6-7 and the North America International Championships on June 12-14.

pokemonChampionsSingleBattleVsDoubleBatt.title: Pokemon Champions Single Battle vs Double Battle
pokemonChampionsSingleBattleVsDoubleBatt.subtitle: Pokemon Champions supports both Singles and Doubles across its main battle modes, but the two formats serve very different needs. Singles is the cleaner starting point, while Doubles is the competitive centerpiece of early official play.
pokemonChampionsSingleBattleVsDoubleBatt.intro: If you are deciding where to spend your training time, the fastest answer is this: learn Singles to understand your roster, but learn Doubles if you want to follow the official competitive direction of the game. Current online competitions and broader VGC-style play are already centered on Double Battles.

items.single: Ranked Battles, Casual Battles, and Private Battles
items.double: Ranked Battles, Casual Battles, Private Battles, and current official online competitions

items.single: Build a team of six, then choose four after seeing the opposing team
items.double: Build a team of six, then choose four after seeing the opposing team

items.single: Supported in online play, but not the main tournament-facing spotlight of the launch period
items.double: The standard competitive format used by the Warm-Up Challenge and Global Challenge 2026 I

items.single: Cleaner reads and fewer active variables each turn
items.double: More active threats, more target choices, and more partner-based planning each turn

items.single: More direct emphasis on one-on-one matchups, speed control, and switching discipline
items.double: Much higher value on partner synergy, positioning, spread pressure, and support options

items.single: No dedicated 6v6 queue is offered in Champions
items.double: No dedicated 6v6 queue is offered in Champions

items.single: Players learning fundamentals or testing individual Pokemon performance
items.double: Players preparing for official events, ladder trends, and broader competitive play

pokemonChampionsBattlePassAndPremiumGuid.title: Pokemon Champions Battle Pass and Premium Guide
pokemonChampionsBattlePassAndPremiumGuid.subtitle: Pokemon Champions splits paid progression into four lanes: the standard Battle Pass, the Premium Battle Pass, the one-time Starter Pack, and the recurring Membership. The real difference is whether you want seasonal cosmetics, instant roster acceleration, or long-term account convenience.
pokemonChampionsBattlePassAndPremiumGuid.intro: For most players, the easiest way to think about these offers is simple. The Battle Pass is your seasonal reward track, the Premium Battle Pass is the expanded version of that track, the Starter Pack is an instant roster-building jump, and Membership is the ongoing convenience layer for serious team builders.

items.value: Seasonal rewards that reset each season
items.examples: Meganium, Dragoninite, Meganiumite, Quick Coupon x36, Teammate Ticket x4, Training Ticket x4, and 10,000 VP
items.bestFor: Players who want steady rewards without buying in

items.access: Paid upgrade to the seasonal Battle Pass
items.value: More seasonal rewards, exclusive clothing, and extra premium items
items.examples: Emboar, Feraligatr, Emboarite, Feraligite, extra tickets, and Pokemon Legends: Z-A main-character outfits
items.bestFor: Players who want the fullest seasonal reward track each month

items.value: Faster team building and training from day one
items.examples: Storage for 50 additional Pokemon, 30 Teammate Tickets, 50 Training Tickets, and the Battle! Trainer Battle song
items.bestFor: Players who want to expand their roster and training resources immediately

items.value: Long-term account convenience for frequent battlers
items.examples: More Box storage, more usable Battle Teams, membership-exclusive missions, and membership-exclusive battle songs
items.bestFor: Players who rotate many teams and play the game regularly

items.access: Applies to select Premium Battle Pass items
items.value: Extra flexibility if you miss a pass reward but still grind battles
items.examples: Some Premium Battle Pass rewards such as Mega Stones can also be purchased from the Shop with VP
items.bestFor: Players who want another route to key battle items without paying for the pass

pokemonChampionsPrivateBattlesAndOnlineC.title: Pokemon Champions Private Battles and Online Competitions
pokemonChampionsPrivateBattlesAndOnlineC.subtitle: Pokemon Champions supports quick friend battles, custom rooms, and structured online competitions from the same Battle menu. The current live event flow also makes it easy to jump straight from private matches into official tournament play.
pokemonChampionsPrivateBattlesAndOnlineC.intro: This section works like a practical launch checklist. It shows how to create or join a Private Battle room, how competition entry works, and which official events matter first if you want to play beyond casual friend matches.

items.title: Finish the setup requirement first
items.details: Complete the tutorial and create a battle team before you can battle other players online.

items.title: Open the Private Battle menu
items.details: From the title screen, press A, choose Battle, then select Private Battles.

items.title: Host a room for friends
items.details: Choose Make a Room. The host can invite friends through the in-game friend list or share the Room ID manually.

items.title: Join a room by invite or Room ID
items.details: Join by accepting an invitation or by selecting Enter a Room ID and typing in the code shared by the host.

items.title: Know the room limits before you start
items.details: Private Battle rooms support up to 12 players. Up to 8 can be battling at one time, with two players per battle, and others can spectate.

items.title: Ready up and begin the match
items.details: Once there are enough players, each battler chooses a spot, selects a battle team, and confirms Ready to begin.

items.title: Enter an Online Competition from the Battle menu
items.details: Go to Battle > Online Competitions, pick an event, review the details, accept the rules, choose your team, and confirm entry. When the event becomes active, return to that competition and begin matchmaking.

items.title: Use Competition Mode for elimination events
items.details: For elimination-style events, return to the title screen, open Competition Mode, enter your Entry ID and Table ID, then confirm when you are ready to battle.

items.title: Play the Warm-Up Challenge while it is live
items.details: The Warm-Up Challenge is the first official in-game competition. Entry opened April 8, battles run April 10-13, 2026. Uses Regulation M-A in an International Double Battle format with 15 battles per day. Participation rewards include Gardevoir and 100 Quick Coupons.

items.title: Mark down the next big competition
items.details: Global Challenge 2026 I opens registration on April 23 and closes May 4, 2026 at 01:59 UTC. Battles run May 1-4, 2026. Uses Regulation M-A, listed as an International Double Battle event with 15 battles per day.

items.title: Follow the shift into official circuit play
items.details: Pokemon Champions becomes the platform for Play! Pokemon Premier Events starting with the Indianapolis Regional Championships on May 29-31, 2026, and remains the standard platform for later Premier Events.

faq.title: Frequently Asked
faq.subtitle: Everything you need to know about Pokemon Champions

questions.question: What is Pokemon Champions?
questions.answer: Pokemon Champions is a free-to-start competitive battle game for Nintendo Switch and Switch 2, launched on April 8, 2026. It features Ranked, Casual, and Private Battles in Singles and Doubles formats, with Mega Evolution and official VGC support.

questions.question: Is Pokemon Champions free to play?
questions.answer: Yes. Pokemon Champions is free-to-start on Nintendo Switch. You can download and begin playing at no cost. Victory Points (VP) are earned through battles and cannot be directly purchased.

questions.question: Can I transfer Pokemon from Pokemon HOME to Pokemon Champions?
questions.answer: Yes. Pokemon Champions supports Pokemon HOME transfers. Only Pokemon that appear in Pokemon Champions can visit from HOME. This is one of the fastest ways to widen your roster from day one.

questions.question: What is Mega Evolution in Pokemon Champions?
questions.answer: Mega Evolution is a battle mechanic enabled under the first Ranked ruleset. The Omni Ring is the in-game battle device tied to Mega Evolution. Mega forms provide significant power boosts and are central to competitive team building.

questions.question: What are the best Pokemon Champions starter teams?
questions.answer: For the best all-around start, pick Gardevoir. For the strongest Singles team, pick Pikachu for its loaded support lineup including Garchomp and Kingambit. For Doubles, Tyranitar gives you the cleanest early game plan with Sand Stream and Intimidate support.

questions.question: Is Pokemon Champions the official VGC game?
questions.answer: Yes. Pokemon Champions is the official VGC software for the 2026 World Championships. Ranked Battles in Pokemon Champions follow competitive regulations, making it the primary platform for official tournament play.
